serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 500ml bottle of Fuze Green Tea, as sold at Dunkin' Donuts Singapore and convenience outlets, providing approximately 150 kcal, 37.5g carbohydrate, and 36.7g sugar per bottle.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beverages, tea, ready-to-drink, green tea, sweetened (Branded Foods)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Fuze Green Tea is a Coca-Cola branded RTD sweetened green tea. USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods entry for Fuze Green Tea (FDCID 2049706 and related entries) reports approximately 30 kcal/100ml, 0g protein, 0g fat, 7.5g carbohydrate, and 7.3g sugar per 100ml, consistent with the input record. Values cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database for sodium. Vitamin C at 10mg/100ml is consistent with USDA Branded entry noting added ascorbic acid. Input per_100g values retained and confirmed against USDA Branded Foods.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweetened ready-to-drink green tea beverages — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweetened iced tea and sugar-sweetened beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Fuze Green Tea in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low, per the standard GI classification boundary of ≤55 = low) based on the closest analogous approved-source entry: sweetened iced tea and sugar-sweetened beverages with similar sugar content (~7–8g/100ml) reported in the University of Sydney GI Database (GI range 47–65 for SSBs). A GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category (≤55). Glycemic load per 500ml serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (55 × 37.5) / 100 = 20.6, rounded to 20.5. Despite a low GI, the GL per full bottle is high due to the large carbohydrate load. Estimation basis documented per Veritas policy.

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar content (7.33g/100ml) in a sugar-sweetened beverage with zero dietary fibre; insulin-driven sodium retention mechanism independently raises blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid Fuze Green Tea as its 7.33g/100ml sugar content exceeds the 5g/100ml SSB threshold; a full 500ml bottle delivers approximately 36.7g of sugar, which promotes insulin-driven sodium retention and raises blood pressure independently of its low sodium content (5mg/100ml); unsweetened green tea is the recommended alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 7.33g sugar/100ml and zero dietary fibre; liquid sugar causes rapid postprandial glucose spikes without fibre buffering, and a full bottle delivers 36.7g sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fuze Green Tea is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes because its 7.33g/100ml sugar content in liquid form is absorbed rapidly without fibre buffering, and a full 500ml bottle delivers approximately 36.7g of sugar (nearly 9 teaspoons), causing significant postprandial glucose excursions; unsweetened green tea, which provides catechins that may improve insulin sensitivity, is the recommended substitute.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar load per serving (36.7g per 500ml bottle) with zero saturated fat or sodium concern; excess sugar intake is independently associated with elevated triglycerides and cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid Fuze Green Tea because the high added sugar content (7.33g/100ml; 36.7g per 500ml bottle) is associated with elevated serum triglycerides and increased cardiovascular risk per AHA dietary guidelines; while the green tea extract provides polyphenols, the added sugar negates cardiovascular benefit, and unsweetened green tea is preferred.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar at 7.33g/100ml promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production, overriding the anti-inflammatory benefit of green tea catechins (EGCG)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Despite the presence of green tea catechins (EGCG) with anti-inflammatory properties, the added sugar in Fuze Green Tea promotes inflammatory mediator production that worsens RA joint inflammation; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should choose unsweetened green tea to obtain genuine anti-inflammatory benefit from EGCG without the pro-inflammatory sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fuze Green Tea (500ml) is a commercially prepared, ready-to-drink (RTD) sweetened green tea beverage produced by The Coca-Cola Company under the Fuze Tea brand. It is sold in 500ml plastic bottles at Dunkin' Donuts Singapore and various convenience outlets across Southeast Asia. The beverage is made from water, green tea extract, added sugar, citric acid, and added vitamin C (ascorbic acid), giving it a lightly sweet, mildly astringent green tea flavour with a refreshing, thin liquid texture. It contains no fat, negligible protein (0.1g/100ml), and derives virtually all its calories from sugar (7.33g/100ml), providing approximately 30 kcal per 100ml and 150 kcal per 500ml bottle. The beverage is classified as ultra-processed and is a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) by public health definitions. A full 500ml bottle delivers approximately 36.7g of sugar — equivalent to nearly 9 teaspoons — with zero dietary fibre, meaning the sugar is absorbed rapidly and causes significant postprandial glucose excursions. Clinically, Fuze Green Tea is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its high liquid sugar load. The green tea extract provides catechins including EGCG, which have documented antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, but these benefits are substantially negated by the added sugar content. Vitamin C at 10mg/100ml (50mg per bottle) provides minor antioxidant support. The beverage is halal-certified, vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is commonly consumed as a refreshment beverage with meals or as a standalone drink. Unsweetened green tea is the recommended alternative for health-conscious consumers.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in Fuze Green Tea?
Can diabetics drink Fuze Green Tea?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values from the input record are consistent with USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods entries for Fuze Green Tea RTD sweetened green tea (approximately 30 kcal/100ml, 7.5g carbohydrate, 7.3g sugar). Values confirmed via cross-reference with USDA Branded Foods. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as no direct Fuze Green Tea GI entry exists. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the analogous GI estimate and the absence of an exact Singapore HPB Foodo entry for this specific branded product.
notes: 1. ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION: fat_g is confirmed zero by product formulation — Fuze Green Tea is a water-based sweetened tea beverage containing no fat-bearing ingredients; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is a filtered liquid tea beverage with no intact plant fibre. cholesterol_mg is naturally zero as this is a plant-derived beverage with no animal-derived ingredients. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, and polyunsaturated_fat_g are confirmed zero by product formulation. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero by product formulation. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (-1): potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific Fuze Green Tea branded product are not consistently reported across USDA FoodData Central Branded entries and are not available in the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI CLASSIFICATION: Glycemic index of 55 is classified as LOW per the standard GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). GI 55 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened iced tea and sugar-sweetened beverages with comparable sugar content; no direct GI entry for Fuze Green Tea exists in the approved GI database. Despite a low GI, the glycemic load per full 500ml bottle is high (20.5) due to the large carbohydrate load (37.5g per bottle). 4. PORTION CONFLICT: The input record contained two portions both flagged is_default=true (500g and 300g). The 500ml/500g bottle is used as the canonical serving size as it matches the product name and Dunkin' Donuts menu listing. The 300g portion has been noted but not used as default. 5. SOURCE NOTE: The input record cited foodo.sg as the HPB source URL; however, the nutrient values are consistent with USDA Branded Foods for Fuze Green Tea and have been attributed to USDA FoodData Central as the primary approved source. 6. CULTURAL TAGS: Input cultural tags (primary_cuisine: Donut, ethnic_tags: Coffee/Donut/Bagel) reflected the Dunkin' Donuts outlet context rather than the beverage itself; corrected to International/Commercial Beverage for the food record. 7. ALIASES SELF-CHECK: Every string value in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. All 16 aliases confirmed consistent.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3 fix: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index value of 55. Per standard GI classification, GI ≤55 = low, GI 56–69 = medium, GI ≥70 = high. GI 55 is at the upper boundary of the low category. This resolves the q4_glycemic_completeness failure identified in the iteration 2 validator feedback.

Iteration 3 fix: Updated gi_source source_match_notes to explicitly document that GI 55 is classified as low per the standard ≤55 boundary, and clarified that despite a low GI, the GL per full bottle is high (20.5) due to the large carbohydrate load.
